findings.description: attempt is reset to 0 on every loop iteration, so 'attempt < MAX_RETRIES' is always true and a persistently failing job spins forever (infinite loop / no retry cap).
subprocess.run with shell=True interpolates untrusted job.payload 'source'/'dest' into the command string, enabling shell command injection.
Because attempt is reset to 0 before the backoff, RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS*(attempt+1) is always 2.0s and never escalates as the exponential/linear backoff intends.
poll_loop is declared async but calls blocking time.sleep() instead of await asyncio.sleep(), stalling the entire event loop and any other coroutines.
run_with_retries is fully synchronous (time.sleep) and is awaited-less inside the async poll_loop, blocking the event loop for the full retry/backoff duration.
With the default stop_after=None, poll_loop has no termination condition and runs an unbounded blocking loop with no cancellation handling.
asyncio is imported but never used (asyncio.sleep is what the async loop should use); dead import.
list.pop(0) is O(n) per dequeue; a collections.deque would give O(1) FIFO pops for the queue.
sum(values) assumes all payload 'values' are numeric; non-numeric entries raise TypeError which is swallowed by run_with_retries and counted as a job failure.
json.dumps(job.payload) can raise TypeError for non-JSON-serializable payloads, turning an echo job into a retried 'failure'.
On convert failure the captured stderr/stdout is discarded and never logged, so failures are undiagnosable.
JobQueue and the processed/failed counters are not synchronized, so concurrent poll_loop/drain_sync use would race on shared mutable state.
Even ignoring the reset bug, the loop sleeps after the final attempt, wasting a backoff interval before giving up.
drain_sync return type annotation is bare 'dict' while poll_loop counters suggest dict[str,int]; imprecise annotation and inconsistent with typed style elsewhere.
